{"stories":{"featured":[{"story_id":192773,"story_date":1779456527,"slug":"ital-tek-mind-abandon-review","description":"Alan Myson embraces imperfection on his most introspective, human-sounding music to date.","full_text":"Eight albums in, Ital Tek—real name Alan Myson—is a staple of Mike Paradinas’s veteran IDM imprint Planet Mu, and a brief dip into his new record “Mind Abandon” reveals it to be broadly in line with Mu’s core output: a darkly beautiful landscape of distressed beats and melancholic melody, wrapped up in some exquisite sound design. Behind the scenes, though, “Mind Abandon” is far from business as usual. On the contrary, it finds Myson tearing up his own playbook, investing his music with new creative energy in the process.

Before Myson became an electronic musician, he was a guitarist, transforming his instrument’s tone with effects pedals. Making “Mind Abandon”, he returned to the fundamentals of that technique. Challenging himself to compose as much as possible away from the computer, these tracks find him recording his own voice and guitar before processing them into beats, textures, and melodies, working quickly and trying to capture ideas in the heat of the moment.

A sense of spontaneity runs throughout “Mind Abandon”, which often feels like Myson is working instinctually, figuring ideas out in real time. His earliest work for Mu flitted around the margins of the UK dubstep scene, and tracks like “A Hidden Path” and “Undertow” still carry traces of that sound, with their snapping beats and submerged low-end pulsations. But listen closely, and you get a glimpse of the human hand at work. Take “The Hidden Path,” a graceful synthesis of breathy ambient washes and ringing melodies created by Myson plucking at the strings of his daughter’s ukulele. Elsewhere, you hear him drawing from other genres: See “The Pull,” a take on the sinister oscillations of synthwave; or “Kill Switch,” an intemperate mix of gnarled percussion and foghorn drones that comes on like Kevin “The Bug” Martin in a particularly sour mood.

Myson describes this as an introspective record, a way to rethink his approach and embrace a more human, imperfect manner of creation. Perhaps, but “Mind Abandon” is deep and immersive music, the sort of sound you can get lost in. If he’s going inwards, he’s taking us with him.

–Louis Pattison","title":"Ital Tek, “Mind collision of gabber, gamelan, and metal becomes something strangely transcendent on Takkak Takkak’s second album.","full_text":"Takkak Takkak make cultural cross-pollination seem easy. Bursting out of the gates with a thunderous mix of metal, electronica, and traditional Japanese and Indonesian sonics, Shigeru Ishihara and Johanes “Mo’ong” Santoso Pribadi grab the reins of their various wild elements and boldly steer them across a landscape of their own design.

Keen explorers of modern experimental electronic music have, over the course of the last several years, encountered increasingly compelling connections between disparate genres and distinct musical traditions. But there’s something especially potent about the noisy overtones of Javanese instrumentation combined with seething heavy metal distortion and bludgeoning kicks. Building on the groundwork of artists like Senyawa and Gabber Modus Operandi, Takkak Takkak sound utterly unforced as they serve up sonic extremes with equal parts delicacy and brutality. Pribadi’s home-made instruments revel in harmonic distortion, a natural companion for Ishihara’s boisterous production style. But these aggressive qualities are frequently interrupted by intricate, gamelan-coded percussive sequences that take the songs into brooding, meditative terrain.

Bangkok-born, Java-raised and now Vilnius-based, Pribadi has earned a formidable reputation for his dedicated exploration of the traditional music of the Indonesian Archipelago. Along with building his junk instruments since 2017, he has also a member of the collaborative project Raja Kirik. In Ishihara, he found a perfect match; much like Ishihara’s work as DJ Scotch Egg, Takkak Takkak’s live sets are kinetic, explosive affairs. Ishihara came up through the breakcore scene with a punk-spirited, madcap approach to performance that was as infectious as it was confrontational. He’s more wide-ranging as an artist these days, examining his own heritage through the use of taiko drums on“Abu”‘s title track

The first Takkak Takkak album was already a diverse, nuanced affair, but “Abu” finds space for even greater subtlety between the power surges. This tracks with the loose concept the artists set for the music, stating they were trying to depict an actively volcanic landscape to represent the three places the music was recorded: Yogyakarta, Švenčionėliai, and Berlin. There is a sense of non-place about the album, not least on scene-setting opener “Hai no uta,” with its uneasy tapestry of tinkling bells, scooped-out reverb, and disembodied choral tones. It may be the calmest moment on the album, but there’s still a great deal of movement that captures the duo’s grasp of energy and action.

The title track is the most definitive summation of Takkak Takkak, not least thanks to the chugging power chord crunch and guttural growls that punctuate the precisely detailed world building happening around the hooks. It’s a feast of ideas, switch-ups, and spine-tingling tones that should be a jumbled mess. But everything hangs together so bizarrely and so elegantly that it never feels overcooked. When Ishihara’s bass-heavy production starts to flex amidst the deft percussive acrobatics, it moves in step with the drum science rather than dominating it. Even as the balance tips and a gabber kick and trancey synth on “Hino-Tori”—which recalls Ishihara’s early days—the sudden brazen rave tropes feels warranted and welcome.

Some listeners will struggle to listen past the abrasive surface noise that “Abu” leads with, but the subtlety in the music is plain to hear in every sublime rhythmic diversion. It’s a marvel of raw expression and advanced engineering, honoring traditions and embodying the visceral beauty that can be conjured at the crossroads between unlikely combinations of cultures.

–Oli Warwick","title":"Takkak Takkak, Berlin"}}],"audio_stream_url":null},{"story_id":192769,"story_date":1779284588,"slug":"suss-counting-sunsets-review","description":"The ambient country stalwarts tighten up their songwriting while simultaneously expanding their horizons.","full_text":"For nearly a decade, SUSS has been on a fascinating journey through the center of the sound known as ambient country: a journey defined by low speeds, high productivity, remarkable consistency, and the ability to make subtle changes that move their music forward without leaving behind their essential charm. Since 2018, the New York City trio—underground lifers Pat Irwin, Bob Holmes, and Jonathan Gregg—have released five full-lengths, a few EPs and a handful of other releases, shapeshifting slightly but regularly along the way. Their 2018 debut, Ghost Box, established a backdrop for their Eno-meets-Ennio vision—the vast expanses of the American West—and its follow-up, 2019’s High Line, reached past the horizon to sprinkle a bit more cosmic dust onto SUSS’s slo-mo twang. Then, they set out to explore: Meditative sounds on the 2020 pandemic album Promise; more ambient experiments on a series of EPs released in 2021 and 2022; longform drone in collaboration with Australian banjoist Andrew Tuttle in 2023; more moods and more movement on the 2024 album Birds & Beasts, SUSS’s first full-length after the sudden passing of founding member Gary Leib in 2021.

That SUSS’s resume is all over the place stylistically testifies to one of their greatest strengths as a trio: an ability to wander at length without losing sight of their unique place on the threshold between ambient and Americana, tradition and experimentation. Listening to their discography is like looking out the window on a long drive across the prairie states; when the blurry parts slow to a stop, you can tell something has changed, but it’s hard to know exactly what.

Which brings us to the new SUSS album “Counting Sunsets”, released on the band’s longtime record label, Northern Spy. It was recorded more quickly than their previous releases, after a year on the road together, and you can hear both of those circumstances at work in these songs, which are tighter and more focused than most of the band’s catalog. The serene “Sunset I” sets layers of strummed acoustic guitar and swooning pedal steel against a softly glowing tape loop, “Sunset II” is built atop a pulsing synth line that gives SUSS more rhythmic momentum than usual, and the juxtaposed bleep-bloops and twang of “Sunset IX” dance around each other like two partners who find perfect time together, even at different speeds. All three tracks clock in under three and a half minutes long and keep the meandering to a minimum.

Worry not, SUSS stans: the band still finds room to roam. The billowing “Sunset IV” stands apart thanks to its bluesy slide guitar and plaintive harmonica, while “Sunset VI” seems to hover and buzz amorphously for well past five minutes, never quite going anywhere. It leaves that responsibility to the next track, “Sunset VII,” a somber tune that feels like watching flickers of light fade in the rearview mirror on the last ship leaving the solar system. It’s not the album’s closer, but when all is said and done, “Counting Sunsets” offers further evidence that, as gifted as SUSS is at exploration, they’re just as good at getting back home—and they’re helping define a genre along the way.

–Ben Salmon","title":"SUSS, “Counting New York"}}],"audio_stream_url":null},{"story_id":192754,"story_date":1779198827,"slug":"marisa-anderson-anthology-of-unamerican-folk-music-review","description":"The guitarist breaks down musical borders on her latest LP.","full_text":"Americans are increasingly finding themselves stuck behind slamming doors separating them from other cultures and ideas. Guitarist Marisa Anderson’s efforts to counter our current sociopolitical paradigm are all about underlining the crucial, often ignored difference between un-American and anti-American. The latter defines a hostile outsider or inside agitator, but the former simply identifies whatever lies outside our national experience. And as this project makes clear, that’s something to be sought after.

In 1952, Folkways Records released quirky artistic polymath Harry Smith’s incalculably influential Anthology of American Folk Music. The collection’s rare folk, gospel, blues, and country recordings revealed to America its musical heritage and inspired Bob Dylan and countless others to become links to the legacy. Recently, Anderson engaged in some intensive spelunking through Smith’s electronically cataloged record collection at Tulsa, Oklahoma’s Bob Dylan Center. Focusing outward rather than inward, she compiled hundreds of traditional songs from obscure un-American records.

Her aim was to learn, adapt, and record them for her own personal Anthology of UnAmerican Folk Music. In her efforts to undo some of her country’s most firmly entrenched ideas of otherness, Anderson decided to concentrate on music from regions where the U.S. has waged war during her lifetime, like Arabic and Islamic nations and Southeast Asia.

Anderson is best known for the rootsy style broadly dubbed American Primitive. But here, playing by different sets of rules than Western music, she creates a portal where her aesthetic cross-pollinates with the outside world in a freer exchange than what’s usually possible between Americans and those cruelly and myopically labeled “aliens” these days.

The Afghani dance tune “Quodlibet” opens the album, with acoustic guitar subbing for the Afghan dambura. Anderson reels off a lilting tumble of Eastern scales, with an accordion providing the drone underneath it all. From the East African country of Eritrea comes “Rabāba,” a tune for the lyre-like tanbūra. The same melodic phrase repeats over and over throughout the track, with the dynamics coming from the gradually increasing intensity of Anderson’s playing. Put it through a fuzzbox and a tube amp, and it might sound like a Velvet Underground outtake.

With its simple but subtly twisting tune, the Yemeni folk song “Zar” could almost be taken for Celtic, as Gisela Rodríguez Fernández’s violin and viola bounce off Anderson’s perky picking. Deep, reverberant guitar lines and aqueous keyboard clouds take the place of a pair of flutes on the Turkmenia tune “Pair of Duduk,” creating a hazy, half-spooky vibe that leans way into ambient territory.

When the Vietnamese “Whistle Song” arrives to end the journey, we’ve made enough musical stops to get slightly dizzy. Substituting overdubbed electric piano lines for the original recording’s bamboo whistles, Anderson plays into that sweet disorientation, generating a minimalist tableau that feels like a roomful of windup music boxes going quietly crazy. When you’re getting your bearings back afterward, keep in mind that this was only Vol. 1. and there may be still be lots more trips to come.

–Jim Allen","title":"Marisa Anderson, “The Anthology of UnAmerican Folk
